comments.text: This website states that “Erdős and Graham report that Graham, Lehmer, and Lehmer have proved this for $k = 2^i$ for $i \ge 1$, or if $k = -1$, but I cannot find such a paper.” I have also tried to locate this manuscript, but without success. Fortunately, I have just written a short note in which I give a complete proof of the case $k = 2^i$ for all $i \ge 1$, and survey the existing results for the set\[ A(k) := \{ n \ge 1 : 2^n \equiv k \pmod n \}, \]recording all integers $k$ for which $A(k)$ is currently known to be infinite. In short, this problem remains open for every fixed $k$ other than\[k \in \{0,-1,-2, 2^i : i \ge 1\}.\]This note is available on my GitHub page (here). (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

As a follow-up to my previous comment: in my note I included a table listing (Table 1), for each integer $k$, the OEIS sequence corresponding to\[ A(k) = \{ n \ge 1 : 2^n \equiv k \pmod n \}. \]This table is essentially the same as the one collected on the OEIS wiki page 2^n mod n. I am wondering whether we should include these sequences in the ''Related OEIS sequences'' part, since there are $38$ OEIS sequences in total, or instead just add a reference to the OEIS wiki page 2^n mod n, which already collects all $38$ of them. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Hmm, it would be noticeably distorting to have 38 sequences linked, so I have arbitrarily selected the sequences corresponding to $k = -3, -2, -1, 2, 3, 4$ as representative for this problem to add to the database.
